audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman reporting a theft to an officer in a police station.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Uh. What can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I want to report a theft.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I had some things stolen out of my bag yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I'm sorry to hear that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Right, so I'll need to take a few details.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Can I start with your name?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Louise Taylor.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: The woman's name is Louise Taylor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Taylor has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Uh. What can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I want to report a theft.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I had some things stolen out of my bag yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I'm sorry to hear that.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Right, so I'll need to take a few details.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Can I start with your name?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Louise Taylor.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: OK, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: And are you resident in the UK?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: No, I'm actually Canadian, though my mother was British.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And your date of birth?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: December 14th, 1977.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: So you're just visiting this country?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I come over most summers on business.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I'm an interior designer, and I come over to buy old furniture, antiques you know.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: There are some really lovely things around here.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But you need to get out to the small towns.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I've had a really good trip this year, until this happened.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: OK, so you've been here quite a while?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I'm here for 2 months, I go back next week.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: So, may I ask where you're staying now?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Well, at present, I've got a place at Park Apartments, that's on King Street.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I was staying at the Riverside Apartments on the same street, but the apartment there was only available for six weeks, so I had to find another one.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: OK. And the apartment Number?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Fifteen.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I need to take some details of the theft.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: So, you said you had some things stolen out of your bag.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And were you actually carrying the bag when the theft took place?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I really can't understand it.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I had my backpack on, and I went into a supermarket to buy a few things, and when I opened it up, my wallet wasn't there.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And what did your wallet have in it?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Well, fortunately, I don't keep my credit cards in that wallet.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I keep them with my passport in an inside compartment in my backpack.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But there was quite a bit of cash there, about £250 Sterling I should think.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: I withdrew £300 from my account yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But I did a bit of shopping, so I must have already spent about £50 of that.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: At first I thought, oh, I must have left the wallet back in the apartment.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But then I realized my phone had gone as well.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: It was only a week old, and that's when I realized I'd been robbed.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Anyway, at least they didn't take the keys to my rental car.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: So you say the theft occurred yesterday?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: So that was September the 10th, and do you have any idea at all of where or when the things might possibly have been stolen?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Well, at first I couldn't believe it, because the bag had been on my back ever since I left the apartment after lunch.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: It's just a small backpack.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But I generally use it when I'm traveling, because it seems safer than a handbag.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Anyway, I met up with a friend, and we spent a couple of hours in the museum.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: But I do remember that as we were leaving there, at about 4 o'clock, a group of young boys ran up to us, and they were really crowding round us.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And they were asking us what time it was, then all of a sudden, they ran off.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Can you remember anything about them?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: The one who did most of the talking.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Was wearing a T-shirt with a picture of something.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Ah. Let's see, a tiger.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Right, any idea of how old he might have been?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Around 12 years old.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And can you remember anything else about his appearance?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Not much, he was quite thin.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Color of hair?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I do remember that, he was blonde.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: All the others were dark haired.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: And any details of the others?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Not really.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: They came and went so quickly.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: So what I'm going to do now is give you a crime reference number, so you can contact your insurance company.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: So, this is 10 digits, 8795482361.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: So, should I contact the...
audioscript.speaker: LOUISE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: CRIME REPORT FORM

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a manager giving a welcome talk to a group of apprentices who are starting their training at the company.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: My name's Janet Parker, and I'm the Human Resources manager.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: We're very happy to welcome you to your new apprenticeship.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: I hope that the next 6 months will be a positive and enjoyable experience for you.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to start with some general advice about being an apprentice.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Most of you have very little or no experience of working for a big organization, and the first week or so may be quite challenging.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: There will be a lot of new information to take in, but don't worry too much about trying to remember everything.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: The important thing is to check with someone if you're not sure what to do.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You'll find your supervisor is very approachable, and won't mind explaining things or helping you out.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You're here to learn, so make the most of that opportunity.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You'll be spending time in different departments during your first week, so make an effort to talk to as many people as possible about their work.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You'll make some new friends and find out lots of useful information.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: As well as having a supervisor, you'll each be assigned a mentor.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: This person will be someone who's recently completed an apprenticeship, and you'll meet with them on a weekly basis.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Their role is to provide help and support throughout your apprenticeship.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Of course, this doesn't mean they'll actually do any of your work for you.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Instead, they'll be asking you about what goals you've achieved so far, as well as helping you to identify any areas for improvement.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You can also discuss your more long-term ambitions with them as well.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I just want to run through a few company policies for our apprenticeship scheme with you.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Most importantly, the internet.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: As part of your job, you'll be doing some research online, so obviously you'll have unlimited access for that, but please don't use it for personal use.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You'll have your own phones for that.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Some of you have already asked me about flexible working.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: After your probationary 3 month period, some of you will be eligible for this.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: But it will depend on which department you're in, and what your personal circumstances are.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: So please don't assume you'll automatically be permitted to do this.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: I want to make sure there's no confusion about our holiday policy.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Apart from any statutory public holidays, we ask that you don't book any holidays until after your 6 month apprenticeship has finished.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Time off should only be taken if you are unwell.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Please speak to your supervisor if this is going to be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You'll be expected to work a 40 hour week, but there may be opportunities to do overtime during busy periods.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Although you're not required to do this, it can be a valuable experience.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: So we advise you to take it up if possible.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Obviously, we understand that people do have commitments outside work, so don't worry if there are times when you are unavailable.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: As you know, we don't have a formal dress code here.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: You may wear casual clothes as long as they are practical, and the only restriction for shoes we have is on high heels for health and safety reasons.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Comfortable shoes like trainers are preferable.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: There's a heavily subsidized canteen on site, where you can get hot meals or salads cheaply.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: Snacks and drinks are also provided.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: So we've decided to introduce a no packed lunch policy.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: This is partly to encourage healthy eating at work, and partly to stop people from eating at their workstation, which is unhygienic.
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: OK, moving on to ...
audioscript.speaker: JANET PARKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: I'd like to start with some general advice about being an apprentice.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Most of you have very little or no experience of working for a big organization, and the first week or so may be quite challenging.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: There will be a lot of new information to take in, but don't worry too much about trying to remember everything.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: The important thing is to check with someone if you're not sure what to do.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You'll find your supervisor is very approachable, and won't mind explaining things or helping you out.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You're here to learn, so make the most of that opportunity.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You'll be spending time in different departments during your first week, so make an effort to talk to as many people as possible about their work.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You'll make some new friends and find out lots of useful information.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: As well as having a supervisor, you'll each be assigned a mentor.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: This person will be someone who's recently completed an apprenticeship, and you'll meet with them on a weekly basis.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Their role is to provide help and support throughout your apprenticeship.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Of course, this doesn't mean they'll actually do any of your work for you.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Instead, they'll be asking you about what goals you've achieved so far, as well as helping you to identify any areas for improvement.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You can also discuss your more long-term ambitions with them as well.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, I just want to run through a few company policies for our apprenticeship scheme with you.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Most importantly, the internet.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: As part of your job, you'll be doing some research online, so obviously you'll have unlimited access for that, but please don't use it for personal use.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You'll have your own phones for that.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Some of you have already asked me about flexible working.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: After your probationary 3 month period, some of you will be eligible for this.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: But it will depend on which department you're in, and what your personal circumstances are.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: So please don't assume you'll automatically be permitted to do this.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: I want to make sure there's no confusion about our holiday policy.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Apart from any statutory public holidays, we ask that you don't book any holidays until after your 6 month apprenticeship has finished.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Time off should only be taken if you are unwell.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Please speak to your supervisor if this is going to be a problem.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You'll be expected to work a 40 hour week, but there may be opportunities to do overtime during busy periods.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Although you're not required to do this, it can be a valuable experience.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: So we advise you to take it up if possible.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Obviously, we understand that people do have commitments outside work, so don't worry if there are times when you are unavailable.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: As you know, we don't have a formal dress code here.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: You may wear casual clothes as long as they are practical, and the only restriction for shoes we have is on high heels for health and safety reasons.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Comfortable shoes like trainers are preferable.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: There's a heavily subsidized canteen on site, where you can get hot meals or salads cheaply.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: Snacks and drinks are also provided.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: So we've decided to introduce a no packed lunch policy.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: This is partly to encourage healthy eating at work, and partly to stop people from eating at their workstation, which is unhygienic.
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: OK, moving on to ...
utterances.speaker: JANET PARKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO pieces of advice for the first week of an apprenticeship does the manager give?
Which TWO things does the manager say mentors can help with?

text: What does the manager say about each of the following aspects of the company policy for apprentices?
textReadable: What does the manager say about each of the following aspects of the company policy for apprentices?

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two urban planning students called Carla and Rob discussing their presentation on cities built by the Sea, following instructions from their tutor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so what I'd like you to do now is to talk to your partner about your presentations on urban planning.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You should have done most of the reading now, so I'd like you to share your ideas.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And talk about the structure of your presentation and what you need to do next.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, Rob, I'm glad we chose quite a specific topic, cities built next to the sea.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: It made it much easier to find relevant information.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Yeah, and cities are growing so quickly.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I mean we know that more than half the world's population lives in cities now.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, though that's all cities, not just ones on the coast, but most of the biggest cities are actually built by the sea.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: I'd not realized that before.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Nor me.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And what's more, a lot of them are built at places where rivers come out into the sea, but apparently this can be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Why?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Well, as the city expands, agriculture and industry tend to spread further inland along the rivers.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And so agriculture moves even further inland up the river.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That's not necessarily a problem, except it means more and more pollutants are discharged into the rivers.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So these are brought downstream to the cities.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Did you read that article about Miami on the east coast of the USA?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Well, apparently back in the 1950s, they built channels to drain away the water in case of flooding.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Sounds sensible.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Yeah, they spent quite a lot of money on them, but what they didn't take into account was global warming.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So they built the drainage channels too close to sea level, and now sea levels are rising, they're more or less useless.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: If there's a lot of rain, the water can't run away.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: There's nowhere for it to go.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The whole design was faulty.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So, what are the authorities doing about it now?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: I don't know.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I did read that they're aiming to stop disposing of waste water into the ocean over the next 10 years.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: But that won't help with flood prevention now, will it?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: No, really, they just need to find the money for something to replace the drainage channels, in order to protect against flooding now.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: But in the long term, they need to consider the whole ecosystem.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Really though, coastal cities can't deal with their problems on their own, can they?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: I mean, they've got to start acting together at an international level, instead of just doing their own thing.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The thing is, everyone knows what the problems are, and environmentalists have a pretty good idea of what we should be doing about them.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So they should be able to work together to some extent.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: But it's going to be a long time before countries come to a decision on what principles they're prepared to abide by.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, if they ever do.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, I think we've probably got enough for our presentation.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: It's only 15 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: OK, so I suppose we'll begin with some general historical background about why coastal cities were established.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: But we don't want to spend too long on that, the other students will already know a bit about it.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: It's all to do with communications and so on.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, we should mention some geographical factors, uh, things like wetlands and river estuaries and coastal erosion and so on.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: We could have some maps of different cities with these features marked, on a handout you mean?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Or some slides everyone can see.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, that'd be better.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: It'd be good to go into past mistakes in a bit more detail.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Did you read that case study of the problems there were in New Orleans with flooding a few years ago?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Uh. We could use that as the basis for that part of the talk.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: I don't think the other students will have read it, but they'll remember hearing about the flooding at the time.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Hmm, OK.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So that's probably enough background.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So, then we'll go on to talk about what action's being taken to deal with the problems of coastal cities.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: What else do we need to talk about?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Maybe something on future risks, looking more at the long term, if populations continue to grow.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yeah, we'll need to do a bit of work there.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: I haven't got much information, have you?
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: No, we'll need to look at some websites, shouldn't take too long.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, and I think we should end by talking about international implications.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: Maybe we could ask people in the audience.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: We've got people from quite a lot of different places.
audioscript.speaker: CARLA

audioscript.text: That would be interesting if we have time.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Yes, so now should we go on?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Carla and Rob were surprised to learn that coastal cities
options: contain nearly half the world's population.
include most of the world's largest cities.
are growing twice as fast as other cities.

items.title: According to Rob, building coastal cities near to rivers
options: may bring pollution to the cities.
may reduce the land available for agriculture.
may mean the countryside is spoiled by industry.

items.title: What mistake was made when building water drainage channels in Miami in the 1950s?
options: There were not enough of them.
They were made of unsuitable materials.
They did not allow for the effects of climate change.

items.title: What do Rob and Carla think that the authorities in Miami should do immediately?
options: pay for a new flood prevention system
stop disposing of waste materials into the ocean

items.title: What do they agree should be the priority for international action?

text: What decision do the students make about each of the following parts of their presentation?
textReadable: What decision do the students make about each of the following parts of their presentation?

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture on renewable energy that uses the power of the sea.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Producing enough energy to meet our needs has become a serious problem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Demand is rising rapidly, because of the world's increasing population.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And expanding industry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Burning fossil fuels like gas, coal and oil, seriously damages the environment, and they'll eventually run out.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For a number of years now, scientists have been working out how we can derive energy from renewable sources such as the sun and wind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Without causing pollution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, I'll outline marine renewable energy, also called ocean energy, which harnesses the movement of the oceans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Marine renewable energy can be divided into three main categories, wave energy, tidal energy, and ocean thermal energy conversion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I'll say a few words about each one.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, wave energy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Numerous devices have been invented to harvest wave energy with names such as Wave Dragon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Penguin and Mighty Whale, and research is going on to try and come up with a really efficient method.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This form of energy has plenty of potential.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As the source is constant, and there's no danger of waves coming to a standstill.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Electricity can be generated using onshore systems, using a reservoir or offshore systems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the problem with ocean waves is that they're erratic, with the wind making them travel in every direction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This adds to the difficulty of creating efficient technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ideally, all the waves would travel smoothly and regularly along the same straight line.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another drawback is that sand and other sediment on the ocean floor might be stopped from flowing normally, which can lead to environmental problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The second category of marine energy that I'll mention is tidal energy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One major advantage of using the tide, rather than waves as a source of energy is that it's predictable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We know the exact times of high and low tides for years to come.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For tidal energy to be effective, the difference between high and low tides needs to be at least 5 meters, and this occurs naturally in only about 40 places on earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the right conditions can be created by constructing a tidal lagoon, an area of seawater, separated from the sea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One current plan is to create a tidal lagoon on the coast of Wales.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This will be an area of water within a bay at Swansea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sheltered by a U-shaped breakwater or dam built out from the coast, the breakwater will contain 16 hydro turbines.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And as the tide rises, water rushes through the breakwater, activating the turbines, which turn a generator to produce electricity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, for three hours as the tide goes out, the water is held back within the breakwater, increasing the difference in water level until it's several meters higher within the lagoon, than in the open sea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, in order to release the stored water, gates in the breakwater are opened, it pours powerfully out of the lagoon,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: driving the turbines in the breakwater in the opposite direction, and again generating thousands of megawatts of electricity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As there are two high tides a day, this lagoon scheme would generate electricity four times a day, every day, for a total of around 14 hours in every 24.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And enough electricity for over 150,000 homes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This system has quite a lot in its favor, unlike solar and wind energy, it doesn't depend on the weather.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The turbines are operated without the need for fuel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it doesn't create any greenhouse gas emissions, and very little maintenance is needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's estimated that electricity generated in this way will be relatively cheap, and that manufacturing the components would create more than 2,000 jobs, a big boost to the local economy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the other hand, there are fears that lagoons might harm both fish and birds, for example by disturbing migration patterns.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And causing a buildup of silt, affecting local ecosystems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are other forms of tidal energy, but I'll go on to the third category of marine energy: ocean thermal energy conversion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This depends on there being a big difference in temperature between surface water and the water a couple of kilometers below the surface, and this occurs in tropical coastal areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The idea is to bring cold water up to the surface using a submerged pipe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The concept dates back to 1881, when...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture on renewable energy that uses the power of the sea.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Producing enough energy to meet our needs has become a serious problem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Demand is rising rapidly, because of the world's increasing population.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And expanding industry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Burning fossil fuels like gas, coal and oil, seriously damages the environment, and they'll eventually run out.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For a number of years now, scientists have been working out how we can derive energy from renewable sources such as the sun and wind.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Without causing pollution.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today, I'll outline marine renewable energy, also called ocean energy, which harnesses the movement of the oceans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Marine renewable energy can be divided into three main categories, wave energy, tidal energy, and ocean thermal energy conversion.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And I'll say a few words about each one.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First, wave energy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Numerous devices have been invented to harvest wave energy with names such as Wave Dragon.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Penguin and Mighty Whale, and research is going on to try and come up with a really efficient method.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This form of energy has plenty of potential.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As the source is constant, and there's no danger of waves coming to a standstill.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Electricity can be generated using onshore systems, using a reservoir or offshore systems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the problem with ocean waves is that they're erratic, with the wind making them travel in every direction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This adds to the difficulty of creating efficient technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ideally, all the waves would travel smoothly and regularly along the same straight line.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another drawback is that sand and other sediment on the ocean floor might be stopped from flowing normally, which can lead to environmental problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The second category of marine energy that I'll mention is tidal energy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One major advantage of using the tide, rather than waves as a source of energy is that it's predictable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We know the exact times of high and low tides for years to come.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For tidal energy to be effective, the difference between high and low tides needs to be at least 5 meters, and this occurs naturally in only about 40 places on earth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the right conditions can be created by constructing a tidal lagoon, an area of seawater, separated from the sea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One current plan is to create a tidal lagoon on the coast of Wales.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This will be an area of water within a bay at Swansea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Sheltered by a U-shaped breakwater or dam built out from the coast, the breakwater will contain 16 hydro turbines.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And as the tide rises, water rushes through the breakwater, activating the turbines, which turn a generator to produce electricity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, for three hours as the tide goes out, the water is held back within the breakwater, increasing the difference in water level until it's several meters higher within the lagoon, than in the open sea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, in order to release the stored water, gates in the breakwater are opened, it pours powerfully out of the lagoon,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: driving the turbines in the breakwater in the opposite direction, and again generating thousands of megawatts of electricity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As there are two high tides a day, this lagoon scheme would generate electricity four times a day, every day, for a total of around 14 hours in every 24.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And enough electricity for over 150,000 homes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This system has quite a lot in its favor, unlike solar and wind energy, it doesn't depend on the weather.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The turbines are operated without the need for fuel.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it doesn't create any greenhouse gas emissions, and very little maintenance is needed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's estimated that electricity generated in this way will be relatively cheap, and that manufacturing the components would create more than 2,000 jobs, a big boost to the local economy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On the other hand, there are fears that lagoons might harm both fish and birds, for example by disturbing migration patterns.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And causing a buildup of silt, affecting local ecosystems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are other forms of tidal energy, but I'll go on to the third category of marine energy: ocean thermal energy conversion.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This depends on there being a big difference in temperature between surface water and the water a couple of kilometers below the surface, and this occurs in tropical coastal areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The idea is to bring cold water up to the surface using a submerged pipe.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The concept dates back to 1881, when...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
